serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Clear Tom Yum Seafood Fried Rice weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant portion as listed on GrabFood.

nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Clear Tom Yum Seafood Fried Rice exists in any approved source. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice dish entries and USDA FoodData Central seafood fried rice composites, cross-validated against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values are retained where consistent with approved-source ranges for comparable dishes. Sodium of 400 mg/100g is consistent with HPB-documented fish-sauce-seasoned fried rice. Energy 166.67 kcal/100g, protein 7.78 g, fat 6.22 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g are within plausible ranges for seafood fried rice per HPB and USDA composites.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Jasmine rice, white, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Clear Tom Yum Seafood Fried Rice. GI estimated at 72 based on jasmine white rice (GI 68–80, mean ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Fried rice preparation with oil and mixed ingredients may slightly lower GI versus plain cooked rice, but the dominant carbohydrate source remains jasmine rice. GI 72 is used as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 90.0) / 100 = 64.8, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 20.0 × 4.5 = 90.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and tom yum paste; a full 450 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste, limit portion to half a plate, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Jasmine rice base has a high glycemic index (~72); 20 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.89 g dietary fibre per 100 g results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 90 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to 150–200 g, increase the proportion of seafood and vegetables relative to rice, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely given the high glycemic load of a standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at 1.78 g/100g and trans fat is low at 0.04 g/100g; seafood components (prawns, squid, fish) contribute cholesterol at 55.56 mg/100g but also provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request less oil in preparation to lower total fat, and note that the seafood components provide some cardioprotective omega-3 benefit that partially offsets the sodium concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves) and seafood omega-3 are beneficial; however, sodium at 400 mg/100g may worsen tissue swelling, and nightshade ingredients (cherry tomatoes, chilli padi) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory aromatics and seafood omega-3 content but should monitor nightshade sensitivity and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clear Tom Yum Seafood Fried Rice (冬阴海鲜炒饭; Thai: ข้าวผัดต้มยำทะเลใส) is a Thai-inspired stir-fried rice dish popular in Singapore's hawker and casual dining scene. It is prepared by wok-frying cooked jasmine rice with a medley of fresh seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish slices — seasoned with clear tom yum paste, fish sauce, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli padi, cherry tomatoes, mushrooms, garlic, and a squeeze of lime juice. Unlike the creamy or coconut-milk-based tom yum variants, the 'clear' version uses a light, broth-style tom yum base that delivers a sharp, sour, and spicy flavour profile without added creaminess. The dish has a fragrant, slightly smoky wok-hei aroma, a firm rice texture, and a bright, tangy heat from the lemongrass-chilli combination. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at 400 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and tom yum paste, making a full 450 g plate deliver approximately 1800 mg sodium. The high-GI jasmine rice base (GI ~72) and 90 g carbohydrate per standard serving make this dish a high glycemic load meal, requiring portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The seafood components contribute cholesterol (55.56 mg/100g) but also provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin C (6.67 mg/100g) from lime and tomatoes.

Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice composites and USDA seafood fried rice entries, cross-validated against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Values are plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against a direct approved-source match. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific tom yum seafood fried rice preparation is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted; estimated ~170 mg/100g was noted in the input but cannot be verified against an approved source and is therefore not populated. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the approved sources for this dish category. 4. The input source listed (FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table) was noted but nutrient values were cross-validated against HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central as primary approved sources per Project Veritas source policy. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine white rice per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 6. The dish is sold under the menu code '722D' by a GrabFood vendor in Singapore; the canonical food name strips the vendor code. 7. All core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 6.22, carbohydrate_g 20.0) are non-zero and consistent with a seafood fried rice dish; no zero-macro justification is required.
